perturbation.original_text: (e) shall protect the com mercial inte rest of the disclosing party and sh all take the sam e precautions to protect the Inform ation as a careful and prudent person woul d be expect ed to employ for i ts own business and confi dential inform ation and, i n doing so, shal l use reasonabl e care t o protect the Inform ation from unaut horised use, harm , expl oitation, m anipulation, m odification, interference, misuse, m isappropri ation, copy or di sclosure what soever, except as specifica lly au thorised by the disclosing part y in writing;
perturbation.changed_text: (e) shall endeavor to protect the com mercial inte rest of the disclosing party and sh all take what they consider to be precautions to protect the Inform ation as a careful and prudent person woul d be expect ed to employ for i ts own business and confi dential inform ation and, i n doing so, shal l use reasonabl e care t o protect the Inform ation from unaut horised use, harm , expl oitation, m anipulation, m odification, interference, misuse, m isappropri ation, copy or di sclosure what soever, except as specifica lly au thorised by the disclosing part y in writing;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall take the same precautions' to 'shall take what they consider to be precautions' introduces subjectivity. It is no longer an objective 'reasonable person' standard. Adding 'endeavor to protect' adds another layer of subjectivity. This weakens the obligation to protect the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc) Regulations 2018 requires reasonable steps to keep information secret. By allowing the receiving party to determine the level of precaution ('what they consider to be precautions'), the modified clause introduces ambiguity and may not meet the required standard of 'reasonable steps' necessary for trade secret protection. The addition of 'endeavor to protect' introduces ambiguity, which conflicts with the need for clear and enforceable measures to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates specific precautions to protect information using an objective 'reasonable person' standard. The changed text replaces 'shall take the same precautions' with 'shall take what they consider to be precautions' and adds 'endeavor to protect'. This introduces subjectivity and weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation states that this creates ambiguity and may not meet the required standard of 'reasonable steps' necessary for trade secret protection, and introduces ambiguity, which conflicts with the need for clear and enforceable measures to maintain confidentiality. Although there are no explicit snippets, the Law Explanation shows that the altered text undermines the necessary legal protections by making the obligation subjective and less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (f) shall keep all In formation that is electronically stored in a tangible form segregat ed from all of its own propert y and i n a safe and secure envi ronm ent and protect and keep safe all such Inform ation from any loss, harm , theft, unaut horised use, t ampering, sabot age, unaut horised dupl ication, dest ruction, damage or interference whatsoever
perturbation.changed_text: (f) shall make reasonable efforts to keep all In formation that is electronically stored in a tangible form segregat ed from all of its own propert y and i n what it deems to be a reasonably safe and secure envi ronm ent and protect and keep safe all such Inform ation from any loss, harm , theft, unaut horised use, t ampering, sabot age, unaut horised dupl ication, dest ruction, damage or interference whatsoever
perturbation.explanation: By inserting 'make reasonable efforts' and 'what it deems to be a reasonably safe and secure environment,' the obligation becomes subjective and weakens the standard of care required. What one party deems 'reasonable' might not meet legal or industry standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR requires appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The modification weakens the obligation to safeguard data adequately. Replacing the objective 'safe and secure environment' with a subjective standard ('what it deems to be reasonably safe and secure') contradicts GDPR’s emphasis on an objective, risk-based security approach.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article 32 hearing - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United States military court hearing This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Article 32 hearing" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( April 2010 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) An Article 32 hearing is a proceeding under the United States Uniform Code of Military Justice , similar to that of a preliminary hearing in civilian law. Its name is derived from UCMJ section VII ("Trial Procedure") Article 32 (10 U.S.C. § 832), which mandates the hearing. The UCMJ specifies several different levels of formality with which infractions can be dealt. The most serious is a general court-martial . An article 32 hearing is required before a defendant can be referred to a general court-martial, in order to determine whether there is enough evidence to merit a general court-martial. Offenders in the US military may face non-judicial punishment , a summary court-martial , special court-martial , general court-martial, or administrative separation. A commanding officer, in the role as court-martial convening authority , will consult with the command judge advocate for advice on case disposition; factors to be considered include, inter alia , the relevant statutory and case law, the seriousness of the offenses, the strength or weakness of each element of the case, the promotion of good order and discipline, and the commander's desire for case disposition. An investigation is normally directed when it appears the charges are of such a serious nature that trial by general court-martial may be warranted. The commander directing an investigation under Article 32 details a commissioned officer as investigating officer who will conduct the investigation and make a report of conclusions and recommendations. This officer is never the ac
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates a 'safe and secure environment' for electronically stored information. The modified text weakens this by stating the party only needs to make 'reasonable efforts' to keep the information safe in 'what it deems to be a reasonably safe and secure environment.' This introduces subjectivity, potentially failing to meet required data protection standards, as suggested by the Law Explanation referencing GDPR Article 32. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly mention GDPR or data protection standards, the perturbation and law explanations indicate a weakening of security obligations, creating a contradiction in environments requiring objective security measures.

perturbation.original_text: (h) shall retu rn to the disclosing party all In formation, copi es and t angible manifestations t hereof, i n any form whatsoever, an d delete all In formation from all retriev al system s and databases o r destroy the same immediately upon t he direction of t he disclosing part y, and furni sh to the disclosing part y, immediately upon i ts request , a cert ificate of a seni or offi cer or di rector attesting to such ret urn, deletion or dest ruction, as t he case m ay be;
perturbation.changed_text: (h) shall retu rn to the disclosing party all In formation, copi es and t angible manifestations t hereof, i n any form whatsoever, an d delete all In formation from all retriev al system s and databases o r reasonably attempt to destroy the same upon t he direction of t he disclosing part y, and use reasonable efforts to furni sh to the disclosing part y, upon its request, a cert ificate of a seni or offi cer or di rector attesting to such ret urn, deletion or dest ruction, as t he case m ay be;
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'reasonably attempt to destroy' and 'use reasonable efforts to furnish', the obligation becomes weaker and less definitive. The receiving party is not required to completely destroy the information, and they only need to use reasonable efforts to provide a certificate.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 requires reasonable steps to keep information secret, which implies complete destruction or return upon request and definite confirmation of such action. Changing 'destroy' to 'reasonably attempt to destroy' introduces ambiguity about whether destruction will actually occur and weakens the measures needed to protect the trade secret. Likewise, if the receiving party only uses 'reasonable efforts' to furnish the certificate, there is no guarantee that any documentation or evidence of compliance will be given, undermining the disclosing party's right to confirmation of return, deletion or destruction of information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to destroy information and furnish a certificate, which contradicts the principle of maintaining secrecy and ensuring compliance. The original text requires immediate destruction and a certificate of such action, while the changed text only requires a 'reasonable attempt' to destroy and 'reasonable efforts' to furnish the certificate. This weakening undermines the disclosing party's right to confirmation of return, deletion, or destruction of information. Though the snippets do not directly address Trade Secrets, the Law Explanation connects the changes to Regulation 3, requiring reasonable steps to keep information secret, implying complete destruction or return upon request.
